title: Date and Time Data
text: Date and Time Data Uniface offers a wide variety of data types for date and time values. date time datetime lineardate lineartime lineardatetime The linear data types contain an elapsed date or time. Internally, Uniface handles the values for all Date and Time data types as double-precision float values. This value is always the number of days since the base date of 1-JAN-0000 00:00:00, using days as the unit of measurement. Therefore, the integer part of this value represents the day and the fraction represents the time part of the day. A Date value (that is, with no Time included) is treated as a combined Datetime value, with the Time set to NULL. Similarly, a Time value is a combined Datetime value with the Date set to NULL. The major advantage of this system is that arithmetic with dates and times is very simple. For example, adding the value 1.5 to a Date is equivalent to adding one-and-a-half days, because for Uniface the value 1.5 means one-and-a-half days. Table 1. How Uniface interprets values as date and time Value Date part is ... Time part is ... 0 NULL NULL 0.5 NULL 12:00:00 1 1-jan-0000 00:00:00 1.5 1-jan-0000 12:00:00 Year 0 is a Leap Year Year 0 is considered a leap year so it contains contains 366 days, including February 29. This is relevant when performing calculations with dates, particularly those involving elapsed times with the results expressed in months. Limits for Date and Time Values The range of values recognized by Uniface lies between 1 January 0000 00:00:00.00 and 31 December 9999 23:59:59.99, inclusive. Any calculation which results in or uses a value outside these limits is an unreasonable calculation for Uniface and the result is not always predictable; it will be almost certainly incorrect. If you enter a value in a field outside these limits, Uniface beeps and refuses to continue until you correct the error. If the result of a calculation leads to a result which is not between 01-JAN-0000 00:00:00.00 and 31-DEC-9999 23:59:59.99 (noting that for Time calculations, the Date part is empty), the result is 31-DEC-9999 23:59:59.99. This result is formatted according to the applicable display format. If the result is not displayed, Uniface does not format the data and the results are unpredictable. Some operations are not meaningful in certain circumstances and should not be used. For example, what would the following calculation mean? $1 = 2 * $date Copy On the other hand, the following construction is reasonable, if the ELAPSED_TIME field contains an elapsed time which is not too large for this calculation and if $1 contains a numeric value: ((4 * ELAPSED_TIME) / $1) Copy Related Links Parsing and Displaying Date and Time Data When Uniface parses the value of a Date, Time, or Dateline field, it uses either Classic mode or NLS mode, as determined by the $nlslocale and $nlsformat variables. Date and Time Constants To create date and time constants, each unit of date and time has its own code which can only be used in combination with numeric integer constants. Display Formats for Date and Time Data Display formats for date and time data typically apply a pattern to the data. For example, you can indicate whether you want a short form of the date or a long form, and whether to use space, slashes, or dashes as separators. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
